Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled Web We Weave

Adventure #5—Lights... Camera... “What in the world have you done to Linda?” Robert demanded with a wry smile, as he joined Tom and I at ourChange the Action
table. “She’s back there typing away like someone possessed.”
Adventure #6—The Write Stuff
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled “Tom,” I said, “this is Robert. He’s in charge of our new website. Robert, Tom is the inventor I’ve been telling you	Web We Weave Previous Lesson Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
about.”Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas
Adventure #9—The Beginning Robert raised his eyebrows as he shook Tom’s hand. “No wonder she’s so charged up,” he grinned. “Hey, can
you come up with some ideas for the store’s web page?”	Postscript... Four Years Later
“Absolutely not.” replied Tom without hesitation, though the smile on his face remained. Robert looked a bit
surprised, but I could see a lesson coming on. Sure enough, Tom continued: “I don’t know the first thing about
web development or design. However, Robert, I can help you come up with some ideas for the store’s website.”
Robert seemed relieved. “Thank goodness. I’ve been drawing blanks!”
As usual, Tom didn’t waste any time. “Let’s start with a new group of boxes that I think might be particularly
appropriate for this—the Change the Design boxes.
Tom clapped his hands together, startling Robert, who wasn’t yet used to his enthusiasm. “First box: Change the
Shape . When I’m trying to solve an engineering problem, I look to see whether the entire system—or any part of
it—could be improved or made more useful by changing it from balanced to unbalanced, or from straight to
curved. Or, of course, the other way around.”
Tom proceeded to clarify: “Two examples. Once, I had to design an industrial mixing device. When I looked in
the Change the Shape box, I realized that designing the mixing bowl to be slightly out of balance would create a
movement and vibration that would facilitate the goal.
“Another time, I was tasked with designing a runway for a small island. The problem was that no usable pieces
of land were long enough to accommodate it. But when I looked in this box, I realized that if I made the runway
curve slightly, I could fit it in the available space.”
“I can’t very well change the shape of our website,” Robert objected. Tom and I exchanged a quick smile.
“Don’t be so literal,” Tom replied quickly. “Think more in terms of associations and metaphors.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 6 --

“Here’s how I see it. Most people, and hence most businesses, like balance and symmetry. If it’s in balance,
that’s good. If everything is equal, that’s good. Equality is ‘the American way.’ Or… it was , anyways. Perhaps
that’s why everyone seems to have a bit of a blind spot about things that might be improved by making them
imbalanced or unequal.
“Take customers, for example. For most businesses, a customer is a customer is a customer. They treat them all
equally, right?” At this, Tom paused for dramatic effect. But what if you didn’t ?”
Robert’s eyes lit up as he realized the implications of what Tom was saying. “Of course! You could treat them
differently online, because you can identify and categorize a customer when they enter the site. I’ve heard that
data analysis can help us to understand if they’re new customers or returning visitors, and whether or not they’ve
made a purchase from us in the past. I’d need to do a crash course or two on how it all works, of course, but I bet
I could pick it up. That could be really useful for us.
“We could offer them different deals,” he added, “depending on how good a customer they are, whether they had
bought recently or not, and based on their special interests.”
“Yes,” I jumped in, “and we could do that without telling the customers. Or , we could openly categorize customers
by buying level—for instance, bronze, silver, gold and platinum. Compared to regular customers, bronze
customers could get better deals and access to an exclusive ‘bronze’ area of our site. Silver customers would
get even more, and so on. It would reward people who bought more and could motivate people to buy even
more.”
“See what happens when you start deliberately throwing things off kilter?” Tom pointed out. “Another thing I find
about most people and most businesses is that they like straight lines. A line, after all, is the shortest distance
between two points.
“Because of this, we tend to think very linearly. Everything is straight lines and direct routes, literally as well as
figuratively. How can you throw your business some curves on your website?”
Robert seemed to understand instantly. “Well, our website is very linear,” he admitted. “You go from A to B to C.
You enter, buy—or don’t buy—and you leave. Perhaps it could be more like a circle. How do I make the visitor
come back—either immediately, if he tries to exit the site, or in the future?”
I resisted my urge to jump in, letting Robert take the glory on his ideas.
“What if,” Robert continued, inspired, “when they try to exit, we have a box pop up with a special discount offer
that’s good only for the next 10 minutes? I’ve seen this done before and it sure works on me. That might stop
customers from leaving. We could offer our online customers discounts on future online purchases. That could
be another good tactic for increasing web sales?
“And what if we offered some sort of giveaway—a lead capture, I think it’s called—in exchange for our web
visitors’ email addresses? We could send them weekly newsletters with special announcements, interesting
book news and special offers.
“There might even be a way to send different emails to different customers based on their purchasing habits. I’ll
need to look into it. That would create a kind of loop that would keep leading people back to the website.”
Tom noticed I was busily drawing on a napkin. “What’s that?” he asked, nodding over to my doodle.
“I’m redesigning the layout of the store,” I replied, without looking up. “You’ve made me realize how linear it is—
just straight lines. People come in, walk up and down some aisles, and then… leave! I realized that if it were laid
out with more curves, I could create a kind of circular path, which would subtly encourage them to cover more
ground and spend more time browsing.”
“Great,” said Tom, nodding his head in approval. “You’ve moved from the literal level to the abstract level. Now,
let’s look in another ‘ Change the Design ’ box: Change the Look . Here’s where you can consider changing
things like color, transparency, visibility or porosity.
“On the engineering level, I love these kinds of solutions, because the results are so, well, visible . Like anything
made to glow in the dark—like the hands of a clock or those little bumps that divide the lanes of highways.
Highlighter pens. Camouflage. Smoke screens. Transparent packaging that lets you see what’s inside. Porous
packaging that lets what’s inside breathe or evaporate moisture if it needs to.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 6 --

“Okay now, Robert, I suppose you’ve heard about the importance of coming up with lots of ideas quickly.”
Robert nodded and winced jokingly.
“You’re on now. Just say whatever comes into your head in response to what I say. Ready?” Tom asked, setting
up the timer on his phone again. “Visibility.”
Robert took a deep breath and plunged in. “We could make certain things on our site stand out more to guide
people where to go next. We could make the website navigation buttons more visible, so people would know
where to go next—especially the buttons that lead them to places we really want them to go, like the ordering
section.”
“Good. Transparency.”
“Maybe we shouldn’t be so discreet about the fact that we’re trying to sell people books. We could say right up
front that we want to earn their business, and that we’re going to do it by giving them useful information that
applies to them on our website and in our newsletters. We’ll say that we want them to get to know us better,
because once they do, we just know that they’ll like us and be more likely to give us their business.”
“Good! Now what about something you would make less transparent? What is something that might be
improved by being more hidden?”
“Let’s see… Well, it would be best to make our online visitors feel like they’re our one and only visitor—rather
than just a number. I mean, that’s how we try to make the in-person experience of shopping at our store, so why
shouldn’t it be the same online? We could do this easily enough online with some personalized touches, such as
using their name if we have it, customizing what they see for their interests and so on.”
“Porous,” Tom threw out next.
“Goodness—now that makes me think of how, right now, our site is filled with holes we need to fill, where people
can just drop out because they got bored, confused or frustrated.
“But in a way,” Robert continued without even glancing at Tom for approval, “it should also be more porous in
terms of multiple entry points. We need more ways people can get to our site—like search engine listings, social
media ads, using the website in our advertising, link building… Wow! I never knew I had so many ideas in me!”
“Most people do,” said Tom. “They just don’t have a good system for getting to them.”
“Well, you’ve certainly helped me get to mine, Tom,” said Robert, standing up to take his leave. “Now if you’ll
both excuse me, it looks like I’ve got quite a bit of re-designing and programming to do. Thanks for helping me
come up with these fantastic ideas, Tom!”
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
In the final two boxes, Change the Shape and Change the Look (the Change the Design boxes), you saw how
changing the look of something can trigger an avalanche of ideas — many of which can be applied to the web.
I do want to re-emphasize here that you can take the boxes both literally and figuratively (non-literally).
In other words, you can use these Change the Design boxes literally by changing things in a physical sense. For
example, you can change the shape or look of an object or design by making it curved instead of straight,
straight instead of curved, more or less balanced, more or less transparent, a different color and so on.
But thinking a bit less literally, you can also change the shape and look of an organization, of people’s attitudes,
of ideas, of writing, of situations and of problems or opportunities.
You can make something more or less straight (forward), go about something in a more roundabout way, throw
something or someone off balance, color or shade writing or an idea differently, make something more hidden
(or clearer) or make it bigger or smaller.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 6 --

And there’s nowhere better to practice these techniques than the internet.
The computers, software and all the people connected by the web are at once both physical and non-physical,
tangible and intangible. That’s why it’s a place where it’s so easy to change the look and feel of things, both
literally and non-literally.
Let’s now enjoy a romp through the two Change the Design boxes together, so you can get a real feel for what I
mean…
Click here to download this fillable PDF
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 6 --

Click here to download this fillable PDF
MODULES
Your Action Steps
1. Notice the look and shape of people, situations, ideas, problems and opportunities. Look for opportunities to
change these things to your advantage.
2. Now that you’ve been through all 15 boxes and know how to apply them less literally, go through them all again
(with the same or a different problem or situation in mind) and try to apply them more freely, more imaginatively
and less literally.
The next Adventure doesn’t contain any boxes. But it does contain some of the most important advice about
creativity and problem solving you may ever receive…
Don’t just think literally. Also think in terms of associations and metaphors. And think on many
different levels.
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
